lessonTitle: He Will Swallow Up Death in Victory
quickInsight: Easter means the worst thing that happens to you isn't the last thing.

quotableVerses.text: He will swallow up death in victory; and the Lord God will wipe away tears from off all faces.

Surely he hath borne our griefs, and carried our sorrows: yet we did esteem him stricken, smitten of God, and afflicted. But he was wounded for our transgressions, he was bruised for our iniquities: the chastisement of our peace was upon him; and with his stripes we are healed.

And he shall go forth, suffering pains and afflictions and temptations of every kind; and this that the word might be fulfilled which saith he will take upon him the pains and the sicknesses of his people. And he will take upon him death, that he may loose the bands of death which bind his people; and he will take upon him their infirmities, that his bowels may be filled with mercy, according to the flesh, that he may know according to the flesh how to succor his people according to their infirmities.

Which suffering caused myself, even God, the greatest of all, to tremble because of pain, and to bleed at every pore, and to suffer both body and spirit—and would that I might not drink the bitter cup, and shrink—

sections.title: The Story
items: Hundreds of years before Jesus was born, Old Testament prophets described exactly what would happen to Him — riding into Jerusalem on a donkey, betrayed for 30 pieces of silver, hands and feet pierced, soldiers gambling for His clothes.
Isaiah called Him 'despised and rejected of men; a man of sorrows, and acquainted with grief' — and then promised He would 'swallow up death in victory' (Isaiah 25:8).
Every detail landed. Zechariah called the donkey. The Psalms called the nails. Isaiah called the borrowed tomb. Then came the empty tomb.
This week is Easter AND General Conference — the one weekend a year where the whole Church focuses on what the Resurrection means for every person who has ever lived.

sections.title: Why This Matters to Them
items: When you bomb a test, lose a friendship, or feel like you don't measure up — the Atonement covers that. Alma 7:11 says He took on 'pains and afflictions and temptations of every kind.' Every kind includes yours.

The manual sorts what Christ overcomes into four categories: sin, death, trials, and weaknesses. For a 14-year-old, that's the stuff you did wrong, the people you've lost, the hard things happening to you, and the things you wish you were better at. He covers all four.
Isaiah 53:3 says He was 'despised and rejected.' Jesus knows what it feels like to be left out, mocked, and alone. He's not coaching from the sidelines — He's been in it.
Romans 8:38–39 promises that nothing — not death, not life, not anything — can separate you from God's love. Nothing you're going through right now changes that.

sections.title: Talk Starters
items: If a friend who isn't religious asked you, 'Why do you care about some guy who lived 2,000 years ago?' — what would you actually say?
What's the hardest thing you've dealt with this year? Does it change anything to know Christ specifically experienced something like it — not just watched it happen?
Why do you think God let prophets predict Easter hundreds of years early? What does that tell you about His plan for your life?
General Conference is this weekend. If you only remember one thing a prophet says about Jesus Christ, what do you hope it is?

sections.title: How to Teach It
items: Read the prophecy-fulfillment pairs out loud — Zechariah 9:9 then Matthew 21, Psalm 22:16 then John 19:17–18, Isaiah 25:8 then Luke 24:6. Then ask: 'If God kept every one of these promises, what does that mean about the promises He's made to you?'

Write 'sin,' 'death,' 'trials,' and 'weaknesses' on the board. Have the boys sort scriptures from the manual into categories (Isaiah 61:1–3, Alma 11:42–45, Matthew 11:28–30, Moroni 10:32–33). Ask which category hits closest to home right now.

60-second hallway version: 'Easter isn't about chocolate eggs. A thousand years before Jesus died, prophets wrote down exactly what would happen. He went through with it anyway — for you. That's Easter.'

sections.title: What Would You Tell a Friend?
items: The manual asks directly: 'How would you explain to a friend who isn't a Christian why Jesus Christ is important to you?' This is THE question for missionary prep.
Elder Ahmad S. Corbitt's talk 'Do You Know Why I as a Christian Believe in Christ?' is a solid model — he explains it simply and without being preachy.
Help them practice a real answer. Not a testimony meeting answer — a lunch table answer. Something like: 'I believe someone took on every hard thing I'll ever face so I don't have to carry it alone.'
This is the kind of thing they'll need on a mission. Let them start practicing now.

sections.title: Challenge for the Week
items: Watch at least one session of General Conference this Easter weekend. Write down one thing a prophet or apostle says about Christ that hits you personally.

Pick one person having a rough time — a friend, a family member, anyone. Text them a scripture about the Savior's peace. Try Isaiah 25:8, Psalm 30:5, or John 14:27. The manual says to be prayerful about who needs it.
Before you fall asleep Easter night, ask yourself one question: 'Do I actually believe He rose?' Your honest answer matters more than anything else this week.
